title: Scope & Closures
summary: Scope decides where a variable can be seen.
Variables inside a function are private to it.
A closure lets a function remember variables from where it was made.
This is a powerful and common JavaScript idea.

sections.text: Not every variable is visible everywhere. Where you create a variable decides who can see it. Understanding this, called scope, prevents a whole class of confusing bugs, and leads to a clever idea called closures.

sections.title: What Scope Is
sections.text: Scope is the area where a variable can be used. A variable made inside a function lives only inside that function. Code outside cannot see it. This keeps different parts of your program from stepping on each other.

sections.title: Closures
sections.text: Here is the clever part. A function remembers the variables that were around when it was created, even after that outer code has finished. This memory is called a closure. It lets a function keep private information that only it can reach.

media.alt: An inner function still reaching variables from its outer function

sections.title: Rooms in a House
sections.text: Scope is like rooms in a house. Something left in the bedroom is not visible from the kitchen. A closure is like a person who steps out of a room but still remembers what was inside it, and can use that memory later, even though no one else can see in.

media.alt: A person leaving a room but remembering what was inside

sections.title: Thinking About Scope
items: Notice where each variable is created.
A variable in a function is private to that function.
Inner functions can see the variables around them.
They keep that access even after the outer function ends.
That kept access is what we call a closure.

sections.text: Closures are how a counter can keep its own private count that nothing else can touch or reset. Many real features rely on this quietly.

terms.definition: The area where a variable can be used.
A function remembering variables from where it was made.

options: Where a variable can be seen
questions.explanation: Scope decides where in your code a variable can be used.

questions.text: Where does a variable made inside a function live?
questions.explanation: A variable made inside a function is private to that function.
